title: Basic match
instruction:
`match` compares a value against patterns and runs the first matching arm.

Every possible case must be covered.

Run this.
successMessage: _ is the catch-all pattern. Without it, the compiler would complain that not all cases are handled.

title: Matching ranges
instruction:
Use `..=` to match a range of values in a pattern.

Run this.
successMessage: Range patterns 90..=100 match any value in that inclusive range. Elegant and readable.

title: Multiple patterns with |
instruction:
Use `|` to match multiple patterns in one arm.

Run this.
successMessage: | separates alternative patterns in a single arm. Both patterns must be the same type.

title: Destructuring tuples
instruction:
Match and destructure a tuple in one go.

Run this.
successMessage: Pattern (x, y) both matches and destructures in one step. The variables x and y are bound to the tuple elements.

title: Destructuring structs
instruction:
Match fields of a struct by name.

Run this.
successMessage: Struct patterns match specific field values and bind the rest. Unmatched fields use the field name as the variable name.

title: Match guards
instruction:
Add an `if` condition to a pattern arm — a guard:

Run this.
successMessage: Guards add conditions beyond what patterns alone can express. The variable x is bound first, then the guard is checked.

title: @ bindings
instruction:
Bind a name to the matched value while also testing a pattern:

Run this.
successMessage: @ binds the matched value to x while the range pattern tests it. You get both the check and the variable.

title: while let
instruction:
`while let` loops as long as a pattern matches.

Run this.
successMessage: while let Some(top) = stack.pop() loops until pop returns None (empty stack). Pattern matching controls the loop condition.
